speaker: interviewer
text: 25, 1950-- 1996. We're conducting an interview with the survivor, Mr. Paul Kuttner. Mr. Kuttner, you just began to tell us about speaking-- you went to Italy, and you met someone there. And tell us about the context and whom you were talking to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --was in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother had a school friend. And I'd been in touch with her once in a while by mail. She came from Hamburg, just like my mother. And she was married to a Swiss Italian by the name of Romegialli. They lived on Lago di Como, Como Lake, near the Swiss border. And once in a while, I corresponded with them. And one day, Mrs. Romegialli said, why don't you come and visit us for your vacation. And I visited them. And they had a beautiful villa overlooking the Como Lake. And they also had a beautiful daughter by the name of Myrtil. And we fell in love. And we married in Switzerland, Mendrisio, the Italian part of Switzerland called-- a little village called Mendrisio. And we lived happily ever after, at least for about five years, until we got divorced. And when she came to this country, she worked for Charles of the Ritz. And I had another job. And-- but unfortunately, it ended in a divorce. It was a very peaceful separation. And she looked a little bit like Sophia Loren. But that's my loss, not hers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, finally, I was single again. And then, my sister met a girl, a young girl, in Germany when she-- when she visited my aunt in Germany, in-- outside Hamburg in [NON-ENGLISH], by the name of Ursula Timmermann. And Ursula always wanted to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister and her husband, Joe Klimmeck, provided an affidavit for her. She came here. We fell in love. And I married her. And the marriage also lasted for about six years. But we had one son, called Stephen, who was born in 1964.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, while you were married to Myrtil and--

speaker: interviewee
text: And Ursula.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Ursula, what were your jobs? What are-- were you working at?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I worked already for a publisher called Watson-Guptill. Watson-Guptill was actually a publisher specializing only in art books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my job was the salesman in New York, having to go from store to store, bookstore and art supply stores, and selling books. But after a few years, I got fed up with it. And then one day, I went to another publisher by the name of Sterling Publishing Company. And I spoke to the head, the president and founder, David Boehm, and asked him for a job. He said, yes, we need somebody. Because we are now starting a new venture of a book which we want to make well known in this country. And you can start as a publicity director. It's called not

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, OK, fine. And again, I started selling these books. And it didn't go at all. And then, I suggested to Mr. Boehm, maybe people don't know what a superlative is. Why don't we call it records or world records, for the next edition? Because it came out every year.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, OK. Fine. Let's try it and see how it works. And after it was changed to world records-- and I have a book here-- it sold like hotcakes. And today, except for the Bible, it's the best selling book in the world. It's been translated into about 37 different languages. And I think about 80 or 85 million copies have been sold. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given credit for-- for originating the title?

speaker: interviewee
text: He patted me on my shoulder.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the extent of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that-- that was the credit. And the-- the-- the following year, I got a $5 raise.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, listen, I am married, I said to Mr. Boehm. And I was expecting-- my wife was expecting a baby. He said, that's not my fault. [LAUGHTER] I felt like saying, if it was your fault, I'd punch you in the face. [LAUGHTER] But in any case, it was very hard at first. But I'm still working for the same publisher. And thank god, the raises are a little bit more than $5 a week now.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- how-- how did you take care of your son after the divorce? What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my son-- well, we were married for, as I said, for about five or six years. And-- until about 19-- I think it was 1969 or 1970. I cannot remember the exact year. And she was working for Carl Zeiss, the opticians here in New York. And they moved-- when we started to split apart, they moved to Washington. Or to Silver Spring, Maryland, rather.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Ursula said, do you mind if I move with you? I'm-- my marriage doesn't work out and I would like to move. And they accepted it right away and she moved to Maryland.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got divorced. I visited my son constantly, every two weeks or so, flew up to-- flew out to Washington. And then to Maryland. And she got married again, twice, twice more. And now she's happily married and lives somewhere in Virginia, Ursula. But we're still in touch. And of course, I am in touch, constantly in touch, with my son. And we visit each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where does your son live?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lives in Washington.

speaker: interviewer
text: DC?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Washington, DC. And he--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: He works in the computer field, selling computers and repairing them and so on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, what do you do with your life, then, afterwards? After the divorce and when you were at Sterling Publishing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you do something else yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yes. What I did, actually, is I started writing books. And I-- at first, I had great difficulty selling my books, but-- as you always do at first. But then I actually asked Sterling Publishing if they would sell a novel I had written, called And they said, no. We do not publish novels. Which is true. They had never published fiction and have never published fiction since then. But, they said, OK. And since you work for us, and we like you and want to keep you, we will try it and see what happens. And it sold about 60,000 copies, this novel. And it was translated into Spanish, became a bestseller there. And I can show you the book later. And so I was quite happy. So the next books that I wrote were much easier to sell. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they also fiction?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Some of the-- the-- I've written four fiction and four nonfiction book. The fourth nonfiction book will actually only come out this coming November, 1996, and deals with Holocaust deniers, how to answer Holocaust deniers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the previous nonfiction books, what were they about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were about anything. They were a detective story, they were a espionage story, and love story. So it really was a mixture of themes. It's not like some writers always write just detective stories or some--

speaker: interviewer
text: But how many nonfiction books did you write? Did you say four?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nonfiction, yeah. This is the fourth, now, the one. And that's called Because there are so many people who claim it was a hoax and it never occurred. And I had to go to Germany and go to the Bundesarchiv, or archive, as they call it here-- and find the original Nazi documents in order to prove that there was a Holocaust.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when is that book appearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: That will come out in November of this year, in two months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, who is publishing it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a small press publisher called Dawnwood Press. And they are located in New York.

speaker: interviewer
text: If-- when you look back-- as you look back at your life, and also mindful of your heritage of the country you come from and the upbringing, do you have any particular feelings now that you would like to state about-- towards Germany or Germans or anything about the Shoah that you'd like to care to share with us now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was very difficult for me, at first, to go back to Germany. In fact, for literally decades after the war, I did-- I refused to go back. I think the first time I returned-- returned to Germany was 1984 or 1985. In other words, 40 years after World War II ended. And it was actually quite a pleasant surprise. First of all, my two cousins were still alive. They were the ones-- and I also went with my son, Stephen. He was my moral support. And it was a wonderful time. They introduced me to other Germans so that I could see they were not Nazis. I saw in each German, at first, a Nazi. But the war had been over for 40 years. Many of them weren't even born during the Hitler period. And I was pleasantly surprised. And since then, I've been back twice, by myself. I didn't need my son's support anymore. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So your feelings are now somewhat different?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fairer. They-- I would say they're fairer. I don't think I could-- but remember, I also married a German girl. But she was born in 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that Ursula?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ursula. Ursula. You are absolutely right. Ursula was married in 1943, who was violently anti-Nazi, really. Even though she had nothing to do with the Nazis, even as a baby, obviously. But in fact, as I told you, she was married three times. And she became Jewish. She-- even though she was Aryan. She is a very devout, almost Orthodox Jewish girl now.

speaker: interviewer
text: How is your son brought up? Does your son-- is your son aware of being Jewish? Or--

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- well, he's aware of being Jewish. But he is in no way-- just like me, we do not practice any religion. We have seen that religion has done-- has created so much hostility, that our philosophy is to be good to your neighbor or to be good to human beings, to be decent, to be honest. And that, to my way of thinking, is the best religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's also the philosophy of Judaism as well. You're quite right. It is. Yes. Mr. Kuttner, thank you very much for sharing your life with us and your experiences.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a pleasure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Mr. Kuttner, would you please introduce your family, through your pictures, to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is the picture of my grandfather, my maternal grandfather, Professor Dr. Eugene Fraenkel, from Hamburg. The picture was taken approximately in 1920. And he was the head surgeon of the Eppendorf Krankenhaus, the hospital in Hamburg. And he died in 1925 of cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is the medal around his neck?

speaker: interviewee
text: The medal around his neck is in recognition for the deeds he did for Hamburg. I'm not quite sure, actually, when it was bestowed on him. This is a picture of my mother's family. From right to left, it's my uncle, Max, and his youngest daughter Ilse, his wife, Lotte, above him. And then, in the center, my grandfather, Eugene Fraenkel and his wife, Marie Fraenkel. Sitting is Hans Fraenkel, one of his-- Eugene Fraenkel's sons. And at the extreme left, my mother [? Margarete, ?] Margarete Kuttner, and her daughter, Anne Marie Kuttner. And this picture was taken in 1916.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anne Marie is your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, is my sister. On the extreme right, you see my grandfather, Professor Dr. Eugene Fraenkel, and his wife, Marie Fraenkel, in front of their house in Hamburg. And on the left, my mother, Margarete Kuttner. And Margarete Kuttner's daughter, Anne Marie, who is my sister. This picture was taken about 1914. On the right, you see my mother, Margarete Kuttner, who perished in Auschwitz. She was gassed in the last gassing operation in October or November, 1944. In the center is my sister, Anne Marie, who survived the war and lived underground. And on the left is my grandmother, Marie Fraenkel, who perished in Theresienstadt, about 1943. This is a picture of my mother, Margarete Kuttner, before she was married. And her name was Margarete Fraenkel. And this must have been taken at the beginning of this century. This is a picture of my father, Dr. Paul Kuttner, in his uniform in World War I. It was taken about the end of 1914, since he was one of the first officers to win the Iron Cross 1st Class during the first World War. And he was what they call a [GERMAN] meaning that he was a regimental doctor throughout the entire war, from 1914 to 1918, although he was actually a prisoner in Belgium for a year or so.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when your father, later on, worked as a physician, did he have any special-- did he practice any special type of medicine?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was a doctor of-- for interior medicine and interior illnesses, and just a regular doctor. And-- but he had a stroke in the mid-1930s or in 1937, and could no longer practice, and later died in Theresienstadt. Actually me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, that's you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is my mother carrying me, Paul Kuttner. My mother-- that was taken around 1923, I suppose, when I was about six months old. And at that time, my father was the doctor in charge at UFA studios.

speaker: interviewer
text: What What were UFA studios?

speaker: interviewee
text: UFA studios.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the UFA studios were the largest movie studios in-- outside Berlin, in Germany. This is a picture of me, Paul Kuttner, with my sister Anne Marie Kuttner, taken in Berlin about 1928 or 1929, when I was four years old and she was 14. This picture shows my sister, Anne Marie Kuttner and me, Paul Kuttner, when I was about two years old and she was about 12. It was taken in Berlin in a photo studio. This photo shows me, Paul Kuttner, at the age of six, in Berlin. This is one of the saddest picture of my life. Because it shows my mother on the right, Margarete Kuttner, and my grandmother, Marie Fraenkel, shortly before they were deported from Hamburg to their respective annihilation camps. My mother was arrested in about 1942 or '43, in Berlin. And my mother, right after the-- she left her mother. And my grandmother, Marie Fraenkel, was arrested the same year and later died in Theresienstadt. This picture shows my mother, on the left, and my sister, on the right, in their last Berlin apartment, in the Uhlandstrasse. My mother was arrested shortly after this picture was taken. And my sister, Anne Marie, went underground almost the-- a day or two after it was photographed. This picture shows Leslie Howard, the well-known actor of movies and theater, in his Beverly Hills home. It was inscribed to me by Leslie Howard, because he became a friend of mine. I met him at Sir Eustice Pullbrook's parties once in awhile. And we became close friends. And he helped me and introduced me to the movie life in England, especially at the Denham Studios. And unfortunately, he was killed in 1943, about a year afterwards--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: --after this picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this picture inscribed?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was inscribed in 1942. A year later he was killed. This is a certificate of my sister, for my sister. Opfer des Faschismus, meaning, victim of fascism. And it was issued to her in 1946 and helped her get special rations in Berlin, immediately after the end of World War II. This is a picture of my sister, Anne Marie Kuttner, and her husband, Joe Klimmeck, and their dog, Rusty, taken in New York around 1952 or '53. Joe Klimmeck is the son of the lady, Mrs. Klimmeck, who helped to save my sister's life by hiding her in her house and in other parts of Berlin during World War II. This is a picture of my son, Stephen Kuttner, taken in 1994 in-- on his balcony in Silver Spring, Maryland. He was-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'm sorry. He was 32 years old at the time. These are the four novels I have written. The first one I wrote, in 1977, followed by and the last novel I wrote was This is a series of three paperbacks I had to write for Henry Holt, the publisher. and in the 1990s. This is the proof of my latest book, And it deals with people who now deny there was a Holocaust. And I had to travel to Germany to prove that there was a Holocaust, by going to various research libraries. And this book really should kill, once and for all, these deniers.
